DATASTRUCT.DATAITEM: This image is in the public domain; posted on CelebsLists.

AUTHORSUMMARY.P: Best known as an actress, Nell Shipman was also an adept writer in many genres, including fiction, journalism, and film scripts.

FRONT.P: Entry written by Daryn Wright and Karyn Huenemann

AUTHORSUMMARY.HEADING: Nell Shipman (1892-1970)
AUTHORSUMMARY.P: Born in Victoria, BC, on 25 October 1892, Helen Foster Barham was an actress, producer, director, and author. Her parents, Arnold Foster Barham (1855-1919) and Rose Clinton Winterbotham (1864-1918) were English immigrants. When Nell was thirteen, the family moved to Seattle, WA, where Nell landed a role in a stage production of At Yale with Paul Gilmore’s travelling theatre company.

By the age of twenty-two, Nell was a well-known and successful actress. In 1910, when she was only eighteen, Nell married producer Ernest Shipman (1871-1931). They had one son, Barry Shipman (1912-1994). Shortly after their marriage, Nell and Ernest moved to Hollywood, CA, where they worked on films together. Their first, God’s Country and the Woman (1916) was a huge success.

Nell starred in several other films, including Back to God’s Country (1919), which was adapted by Nell from a short story called Wapi the Walrus by James Oliver Curwood; the film was produced by her and Ernest’s own production company. In addition to being one of the most successful Canadian silent films of the era, the film portrayed Nell as a heroine who rescues a man in distress. It also contained the first nude scene by a well-known actress.

Nell spent the 1920s working as a director, producer, and writer on several films, often focusing on wilderness and adventure. An advocate for fair treatment of animals, she also owned and cared for approximately one hundred animals, some of which were featured in her films. In 1920, Nell established Nell Shipman Productions, which was nominally successful but went bankrupt in 1924 and was dissolved as a corporation in 1925.

Having been separated for a number of years, in 1920, Ernest and Nell divorced; in 1925, Nell married painter Charles Ayers (1889-1964), with whom she had two children: twins Daphne (b. 1926) and Charles Ayers (1926-2011). During the late 1920s and early 1930s, Nell and Charles were so financially strained that she supported them by writing articles for various magazines, including Atlantic Monthly, Field and Stream, and The Humanist. Nell and Charles separated by 1934.

In addition to her work in film, Nell was an avid writer. Her first work, a romantic thriller titled Under the Crescent, was published by Grosset and Dunlap in 1915 and produced as a film by Universal Studios in the same year. After the bankruptcy of her production company in 1924, Nell spent the rest of her life writing and sending film scripts to production companies. In 1930, McCall’s Magazine published the serialized story Get the Woman ("Mśieu Sweetheart") and Nell's children’s book Kurly Kew and the Tree Princess was published by Dial in New York. Shortly thereafter, she published two works of fiction for adults: Neeka of the North (1931) and Abandoned Trails (1932).

During the final years of her life, Nell worked on a memoir titled The Silent Screen and My Talking Heart: an Autobiography; the book was published posthumously by Boise State University in 1988. A selection of her writings and correspondence was published in 2003 as Letters from God's Country: Nell Shipman, Selected Correspondence & Writings, 1912-1970. Nell died in Cabazon, CA, on 23 January 1970.

LISTBIBCIT.BIBCIT: Under the Crescent (New York: Grosset, 1915)
Get the Woman ("Mśieu Sweetheart") (New York: Dial; Toronto: Longmans, Green, 1930)
Kurly Kew and the Tree Princess: A Story of the Forest People, Told for Other People (New York: Dial; Toronto: Longman, Green, 1930)—illustrated by Elizabeth Ellender
Neeka of the North (London: Collins, 1931)
Abandoned Trails (New York: Dial; Toronto: Longmans, 1932)
Hot Oil (New York: Greenberg, 1935)—with George Palmer Putnam
Milestone Zero (Washington, DC: Kaufmann, 1952)
Mother Liberty: The Gift They Gave Us (s.l.: s.n., 1939)⁸with Elmore Leffingwell
Tomorrow for Sale (New York: Appellate Law Printers, 1941)
The Silent Screen and My Talking Heart: An Autobiography (Boise, ID: Hemingway Western Studies Series, Boise State UP, 1988)
Letters from God's Country: Nell Shipman, Selected Correspondence & Writings, 1912-1970 (Boise, ID: Hemingway Western Studies Series, Boise State UP, 2003)
The Tamiami Trail, various Florida newspapers
This Little Bear Went to Hollywood in Good Housekeeping (1931)
Are Screen Stars Dumb (1928)
Atlantic Monthly (Boston, MA)
The Bioscope (London)
Field and Stream (New York)
Good Housekeeping (New York)
The Humanist (Washington, DC)
McCall’s Magazine (New York)
West Coast Magazine (Vancouver, BC)
Writer, Outwitted by Billy, produced by William Nicholas Selig (1913)
Writer, Actor, The Ball of Yarn, d. by Norval MacGregor (1912)
Writer, One Hundred Years of Mormonism (1912)
Writer, The Shepherd of the Southern Cross (1914)
Writer, The Widow’s Secret (1915)
Writer, The Pine’s Revenge (1915)
Writer, Under the Crescent, Universal Studios (1915)
Actor, God’s Country and the Woman (1916)
Actor, The Fires of Conscience (1916)
Actor, Through the Wall (1916)
Writer, Actor, The Melody of Love (1916)
Writer, Son ‘o the Stars (1916)
Actor, The Black Wolf (1917)
Writer, My Fighting Gentleman (1917)
Actor, The Wild Strain (1918)
Actor, Baree, the Son of Kazan (1918)
Actor, A Gentleman’s Agreement (1918)
Actor, The Girl from Beyond (1918)
Actor, The Home Trail (1918)
Actor, Cavanaugh of the Forest Rangers (1918)
Writes Adaptation, Actor, Back to God’s Country (1919)
Writer, Actor, Director, Trail of the Arrow (1920)
Writer, Actor, Director, Something New (1920)
Writer, Actor, Director, Producer, The Girl from God’s Country (1921)
Writer, Actor, Director, Producer, The Grub-Stake (1923)
Writer, Actor, Director, Producer, short film series Little Dramas of the Big Places (1923)
Writer, Actor, Director, Producer, White Water (1924)
Writer (uncredited), Wings in the Dark (1935)
Writer, Director, Producer, The Clam Digger’s Daughter / The Story of Mr. Hobbs (1947)

FAMILYMEMBER.P: Arnold Foster Barham was born in Bridgewater, Somerset, England, on 11 August 1855. He married Rose Clinton Winterbotham (1864-1918) in 1887 and they had two children together: Maurice Foster Barham (1888-1955) and Helen Foster Barham (1892-1970).
Shortly after their marriage, Arnold and Rose immigrated to Canada. When their daughter Helen was thirteen, they relocated to Seattle, WA. Arnold died on 25 March 1919 in Los Angeles, CA.

Rose Clinton Winterbotham was born on 24 August 1864 in Bridgewater, Somerset, England. She married Arnold Foster Barham (1855-1919) in 1887 and shortly after immigrated to Canada. They had two children together: Maurice Foster Barham (1888-1955) and Helen Foster Barham (1892-1970). Rose died on 7 December 1918 in Los Angeles, CA.

FAMILYMEMBER.HEADING: Marriage:
FAMILYMEMBER.P: Film producer Ernest George Montague Shipman was born on 16 December 1871 at either Ottawa, ON, or Hull, QC. He was married three times before he married Helen "Nell" Foster Barham (1892-1970) on 25 August 1910: first to Adaline Bella Campbell (1872-1893) in 1892; to Effie Elaine Hext (1863-1958) in 1895; and to Agnes Roselle Knott (1865-1948) in 1907. After he and Nell divorced in 1920, he married Sarah McIntosh in 1921.

Following his marriage to Nell in 1910, the two moved to Hollywood, CA, in 1912, where Ernest established himself as a producer, publicist, agent, and promoter. His film, created with his wife as the lead, God’s Country and the Woman (1916), was one of the most successful Canadian films ever made.

Following the success of Back to God’s Country, Ernest created production companies in Ottawa, Winnipeg, Sault Ste. Marie, and Saint John, to some success. He moved to New York in 1931, where he died that year on 7 August.

FAMILYMEMBER.HEADING: Marriage:
FAMILYMEMBER.P: American painter Charles Haines Austin Ayers was born on 24 April 1889 in Canterbury, New Hampshire. He married Nell Shipman (1892-1970) in 1925 and shortly after they had twins, Daphne Ayers (b. 1926) and Charles Ayers (1926-2011).

Charles and Nell spent some time living in Spain before returning to the United States, where they lived in the New England area. The two were in a state of constant financial difficulty, and Nell often wrote newspaper articles to support them while Charles struggled to make a living as a painter. They divorced in 1934 after being married for ten years.

OTHERLIFEEVENT.HEADING: Tangential Information
OTHERLIFEEVENT.P: A play about Nell’s life was written by Sharon Pollock, directed by Brian Richmond, and performed by the Theatre Junction Resident Company of Artists in Calgary in 1999.

LISTBIBCIT.BIBCIT: Nell Shipman Papers (MSS 81), Special Collections and Archives, Albertsons Library, Boise State University, Boise, ID
